evaluation_inputs.system_description: A Learning Management System (LMS) based on Moodle 1.9 (Open Source) used by Universiti Utara Malaysia. It automates the administration of training events and provides an online environment for content creation, delivery, communication, collaboration, and assessment. It includes modules for assignments, chats, forums, lessons, Turnitin, and web meetings, supporting synchronous and asynchronous e-learning activities.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The system was already a fully implemented, operational web-based system (Moodle version 1.9) at the time of evaluation. The evaluation was retrospective, treating the existing implementation as the architecture to be analyzed.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How long will the system be available for a virtual meet’s web cam live chat when there is a network failure?
Can the system authenticate a user if they try to log on with either wrong user id or password?
Does the architecture process any task with the fastest speed?

hardware_view_description: The system relies on network infrastructure susceptible to congestion during high traffic (virtual meets).
Web-based system architecture accessed via internet/intranet.
runtime_constraints: Must support heavy file uploads (Video, audio, PDF, etc.) which was a limitation in the previous system.
Scalability for Virtual Meet module handles 1-100 persons.
Must conform to SCORM (Sharable Content Object Reference Model) standards.

questions: How does the system handle invalid login attempts regarding main page access?
How does the system recover from network failure due to congestion during live video chats?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Entering a wrong user name and password
Clicking an icon to process information with speed
Adding new web pages to my wiki module
Integrating mybox for my file storage
When Network fails during virtual meet and later recovered
The system should be able to handle user errors

business_goals: Enhancing quality teaching and learning within the university community
Managing the delivery and administration of programs of learning via the internet
Provide a cheaper, open-source alternative to the previous system (Learning Care)
technical_constraints: Must be based on Moodle (Modular Object-Oriented Dynamic Learning Environment)
Must be Open Source Software (OSS) under GNU license
Must operate on multiple platforms (Windows, Solaris, Linux, Mac OS, UNIX)

architectural_approaches.description: Moodle's Modular Object-Oriented Dynamic Learning Environment supports pluggable modules (assignments, chats, forums, lessons, Turnitin, web meetings, wiki) enabling extensibility and modifiability

addressed_attributes.rationale: Modular design allows adding/removing features without affecting core system

architectural_approaches.description: Internet/intranet accessible system with centralized server handling requests from distributed clients

addressed_attributes.rationale: Centralized deployment enables consistent access across platforms
Server-side processing handles heavy file uploads and concurrent users

architectural_approaches.description: User authentication system controlling access to main pages and resources based on credentials

addressed_attributes.rationale: Validates user credentials before granting access to system resources

architectural_approaches.description: Adherence to SCORM standards for content packaging and delivery

addressed_attributes.rationale: Standards compliance enables interoperability and content reuse

children.refinement: Response time for file uploads under concurrent load
children.scenario_description: Under normal operations with 500 concurrent users, when multiple large file uploads (up to 100MB) occur simultaneously, the system processes each upload with response time < 5 seconds for 95% of requests

children.refinement: Page load time for course content delivery
children.scenario_description: When authenticated users access SCORM-compliant course content during peak hours, page load time remains < 2 seconds for 90% of requests

children.refinement: System uptime for distributed client access
children.scenario_description: The centralized server maintains 99.5% uptime over a 30-day period, ensuring consistent internet/intranet access for distributed clients across multiple platforms
When unauthenticated users attempt to access protected main pages or resources, the authentication framework blocks access 100% of the time and redirects to login within 1 second
When authenticated users with insufficient privileges attempt to access restricted modules (assignments, forums, Turnitin), the authorization system denies access and logs the attempt within 500ms

children.refinement: Plugin module addition without core system impact
children.scenario_description: A developer adds a new plugin module (e.g., video conferencing) to the modular architecture, completing integration and testing without modifying core system code within 40 developer-hours

When instructors upload new SCORM-compliant content packages from external authoring tools, the system integrates and deploys the content without code changes, completing the process within 15 minutes

When a single plugin module (e.g., Turnitin integration) experiences a failure or crashes, the module isolation architecture contains the failure, allowing other modules (forums, assignments, wiki) and core system to continue operating without degradation

scenarios.scenario_text: Under normal operations with 500 concurrent users, when multiple large file uploads (up to 100MB) occur simultaneously, the system processes each upload with response time < 5 seconds for 95% of requests
When authenticated users access SCORM-compliant course content during peak hours, page load time remains < 2 seconds for 90% of requests
The centralized server maintains 99.5% uptime over a 30-day period, ensuring consistent internet/intranet access for distributed clients across multiple platforms
When unauthenticated users attempt to access protected main pages or resources, the authentication framework blocks access 100% of the time and redirects to login within 1 second
When authenticated users with insufficient privileges attempt to access restricted modules (assignments, forums, Turnitin), the authorization system denies access and logs the attempt within 500ms
A developer adds a new plugin module (e.g., video conferencing) to the modular architecture, completing integration and testing without modifying core system code within 40 developer-hours

When instructors upload new SCORM-compliant content packages from external authoring tools, the system integrates and deploys the content without code changes, completing the process within 15 minutes

When a single plugin module (e.g., Turnitin integration) experiences a failure or crashes, the module isolation architecture contains the failure, allowing other modules (forums, assignments, wiki) and core system to continue operating without degradation

scenario_reference.text: Under normal operations with 500 concurrent users, when multiple large file uploads (up to 100MB) occur simultaneously, the system processes each upload with response time < 5 seconds for 95% of requests

analysis_records.stimulus: Multiple simultaneous large file uploads (up to 100MB)
analysis_records.response_measure: Response time < 5 seconds for 95% of requests
architectural_decisions: Web-based Client-Server Architecture with centralized server-side processing
Server handles heavy file upload processing
Modular architecture isolates upload functionality
analysis_records.reasoning: The centralized server must handle concurrent file uploads efficiently. Without explicit load balancing, caching, or asynchronous processing mechanisms documented, the server becomes a bottleneck. Network bandwidth between clients and server, disk I/O capacity, and database connection pool size directly impact upload performance. The 5-second target for 100MB files requires minimum 20MB/s throughput per upload, which multiplied by concurrent users creates significant resource demands.

risks.description: Centralized server may become bottleneck under concurrent large file uploads without documented load balancing or horizontal scaling
risks.rationale: 500 concurrent users uploading 100MB files simultaneously could exceed server I/O capacity, network bandwidth, and memory resources, causing response times to exceed 5-second threshold

risks.description: Database connection pool exhaustion during concurrent upload metadata storage
risks.rationale: Each upload requires database transactions for metadata storage; insufficient connection pool size could cause queuing delays

risks.description: Network bandwidth saturation between distributed clients and centralized server
risks.rationale: 100MB uploads from 500 concurrent users require substantial bandwidth; internet/intranet capacity constraints not documented

non_risks.description: Module isolation prevents upload failures from affecting other system components
non_risks.rationale: Modular architecture ensures upload processing failures remain contained within the upload module

conflicting_attributes: Performance (larger buffers improve throughput)
Availability (larger buffers consume more memory, risking OOM failures)

scenario_reference.text: When authenticated users access SCORM-compliant course content during peak hours, page load time remains < 2 seconds for 90% of requests

analysis_records.environment: Peak hours with authenticated users accessing course content
analysis_records.stimulus: User requests for SCORM-compliant course content
analysis_records.response_measure: Page load time < 2 seconds for 90% of requests
architectural_decisions: SCORM-Compliant Content Management for standardized content delivery
Web-based Client-Server Architecture for content serving
Authentication framework validates users before content access
analysis_records.reasoning: SCORM content delivery involves retrieving course packages, rendering interactive elements, and tracking user progress. The 2-second target requires efficient content caching, database query optimization, and minimal authentication overhead. Peak hours amplify concurrent request load. Without documented caching strategy or CDN usage, repeated content requests could overload the server.

risks.description: Lack of documented caching strategy for SCORM content may cause repeated server-side processing
risks.rationale: Without content caching, each request requires full SCORM package retrieval and processing, increasing latency during peak hours

risks.description: Authentication overhead on every content request may degrade performance
risks.rationale: If authentication validation occurs on each SCORM content request without session caching, cumulative overhead could exceed 2-second threshold

non_risks.description: SCORM standards compliance ensures predictable content structure and processing
non_risks.rationale: Standardized SCORM packaging allows optimized parsing and delivery mechanisms

sensitivity_points.parameter: Database query response time for content metadata

conflicting_attributes: Performance (larger cache improves hit ratio and reduces latency)
Modifiability (cached content requires invalidation when updated)

scenario_reference.text: The centralized server maintains 99.5% uptime over a 30-day period, ensuring consistent internet/intranet access for distributed clients across multiple platforms

analysis_records.stimulus: Continuous client access requests across internet/intranet
architectural_decisions: Centralized server deployment for consistent access
Web-based architecture supporting multiple client platforms
Modular architecture for component isolation
analysis_records.reasoning: 99.5% uptime allows only 3.6 hours of downtime per month. The centralized architecture creates a single point of failure. Without documented redundancy, failover mechanisms, or high-availability clustering, hardware failures, network outages, or maintenance windows could exceed the downtime budget. Module isolation helps contain failures but doesn't address server-level availability.

risks.description: Centralized server represents single point of failure without documented redundancy
risks.rationale: Hardware failure, network outage, or datacenter issues could cause complete system unavailability exceeding 3.6-hour monthly budget

risks.description: Planned maintenance windows may consume entire downtime budget
risks.rationale: Software updates, security patches, and database maintenance require downtime; without hot-swap or rolling update capability, maintenance could exceed 99.5% target

risks.description: Internet/intranet network dependency creates external failure points
risks.rationale: ISP outages or network infrastructure failures outside system control could impact availability

non_risks.description: Module isolation prevents individual plugin failures from causing total system unavailability
non_risks.rationale: Modular architecture ensures core system remains available even if specific modules fail

sensitivity_points.parameter: Server hardware MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)

conflicting_attributes: Availability (higher redundancy improves uptime)
Modifiability (distributed redundant systems complicate updates and synchronization)

scenario_reference.text: When unauthenticated users attempt to access protected main pages or resources, the authentication framework blocks access 100% of the time and redirects to login within 1 second

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with unauthenticated access attempts
analysis_records.stimulus: Unauthenticated user attempts to access protected resources
analysis_records.response_measure: 100% access blocking with redirect to login within 1 second
architectural_decisions: Authentication and Authorization Framework controlling resource access
Credential validation before granting access to main pages
Web-based architecture enforcing server-side authentication checks
analysis_records.reasoning: The authentication framework must intercept all requests to protected resources and validate credentials before allowing access. The 100% blocking requirement demands fail-secure design with no bypass vulnerabilities. The 1-second redirect constraint requires efficient authentication checks without complex cryptographic operations that could introduce latency.

risks.description: Authentication bypass vulnerabilities if framework not applied consistently across all protected endpoints
risks.rationale: Incomplete coverage of authentication checks could allow direct URL access to protected resources, violating 100% blocking requirement

risks.description: Session fixation or hijacking attacks if session management not properly implemented
risks.rationale: Weak session token generation or insecure transmission could allow attackers to impersonate authenticated users

non_risks.description: Server-side authentication enforcement prevents client-side bypass
non_risks.rationale: Web-based client-server architecture ensures authentication logic executes on trusted server, not manipulable client

non_risks.description: 1-second redirect constraint is achievable with standard authentication mechanisms
non_risks.rationale: Session token validation and redirect operations typically complete in milliseconds, well within 1-second budget

conflicting_attributes: Security (stronger cryptography improves resistance to attacks)
Performance (complex cryptographic operations increase validation latency)

scenario_reference.text: When authenticated users with insufficient privileges attempt to access restricted modules (assignments, forums, Turnitin), the authorization system denies access and logs the attempt within 500ms

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with authenticated users attempting unauthorized access
analysis_records.stimulus: Authenticated user with insufficient privileges attempts module access
architectural_decisions: Authentication and Authorization Framework with role-based access control
Modular architecture with per-module authorization checks
Logging mechanism for security audit trails
analysis_records.reasoning: Authorization requires mapping user roles to module permissions and enforcing access control. The 500ms constraint demands efficient permission lookups, likely requiring cached role-permission mappings. Logging adds overhead but is critical for security auditing. The modular architecture requires consistent authorization enforcement across all plugin modules.

risks.description: Inconsistent authorization enforcement across plugin modules
risks.rationale: Plugin-based architecture may allow third-party modules to implement custom authorization logic, creating potential for privilege escalation vulnerabilities

risks.description: Authorization decision caching may serve stale permissions after role changes
risks.rationale: To meet 500ms constraint, permission lookups likely use caching; insufficient cache invalidation could allow access after privilege revocation

risks.description: Logging mechanism failure could prevent security audit trails
risks.rationale: If logging is synchronous and fails, it could either block the authorization decision or silently fail to record unauthorized access attempts

non_risks.description: 500ms response time is sufficient for role-based authorization checks
non_risks.rationale: Database lookups for user roles and permission mappings typically complete in tens of milliseconds, leaving margin for logging

conflicting_attributes: Performance (longer cache duration reduces database queries)
Security (shorter cache duration ensures timely permission revocation)
Security (detailed logs improve forensic analysis)
Performance (verbose logging increases I/O overhead and storage requirements)

scenario_reference.text: A developer adds a new plugin module (e.g., video conferencing) to the modular architecture, completing integration and testing without modifying core system code within 40 developer-hours

analysis_records.environment: Development environment with existing modular architecture
analysis_records.response_measure: Integration and testing completed within 40 developer-hours without core code modification
architectural_decisions: Modular Object-Oriented Dynamic Learning Environment with plugin support
Well-defined plugin interfaces and extension points
Module isolation preventing core system dependencies
analysis_records.reasoning: The 40-hour budget requires clear plugin APIs, comprehensive documentation, and minimal integration complexity. The modular architecture must provide stable interfaces that don't require core code changes. Testing overhead depends on the quality of plugin isolation and available testing frameworks.

risks.description: Insufficient plugin API documentation could exceed 40-hour development budget
risks.rationale: Developers may spend excessive time reverse-engineering plugin interfaces or discovering undocumented dependencies

risks.description: Plugin interface instability across system versions may require core code modifications
risks.rationale: If plugin APIs change frequently between versions, maintaining backward compatibility could necessitate core system changes

risks.description: Complex inter-module dependencies may require modifications beyond plugin boundaries
risks.rationale: If new plugin requires interaction with existing modules through non-standard interfaces, integration may require core system changes

non_risks.description: Modular architecture design explicitly supports plugin addition without core changes
non_risks.rationale: Plugin-based architecture with defined extension points enables independent module development

sensitivity_points.parameter: Availability of plugin development tools and testing frameworks

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (higher abstraction simplifies plugin development)
Performance (abstraction layers introduce overhead in plugin execution)
Reliability (strict isolation prevents cascading failures)
Modifiability (strict isolation limits inter-plugin communication and feature richness)

scenario_reference.text: When instructors upload new SCORM-compliant content packages from external authoring tools, the system integrates and deploys the content without code changes, completing the process within 15 minutes

analysis_records.environment: Production environment with instructors uploading SCORM content
analysis_records.response_measure: Content integration and deployment within 15 minutes without code changes
architectural_decisions: SCORM-Compliant Content Management with standardized packaging
Content deployment automation without manual code modifications
Web-based architecture for content upload and processing
analysis_records.reasoning: SCORM standards compliance ensures predictable content structure, enabling automated processing. The 15-minute constraint requires efficient package validation, metadata extraction, and content indexing. The 'without code changes' requirement confirms that SCORM compliance eliminates custom integration work for each content package.

risks.description: SCORM version incompatibilities may require manual intervention
risks.rationale: If system supports only specific SCORM versions (1.2, 2004) and content uses unsupported versions, automated processing may fail

risks.description: Malformed SCORM packages may cause processing failures exceeding 15-minute window
risks.rationale: Invalid manifest files or corrupted content could trigger error handling and retry logic that extends processing time

non_risks.description: SCORM standards compliance eliminates need for custom content integration code
non_risks.rationale: Standardized packaging format allows generic processing pipeline for all compliant content

non_risks.description: 15-minute processing window is sufficient for typical SCORM package sizes
non_risks.rationale: SCORM packages typically contain HTML, JavaScript, and media files that can be extracted and indexed within minutes

sensitivity_points.parameter: Server processing capacity during content upload

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (strict validation prevents deployment of malformed content)
Modifiability (lenient validation allows broader content compatibility)

scenario_reference.text: When a single plugin module (e.g., Turnitin integration) experiences a failure or crashes, the module isolation architecture contains the failure, allowing other modules (forums, assignments, wiki) and core system to continue operating without degradation

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with active plugin modules
analysis_records.stimulus: Single plugin module failure or crash
analysis_records.response_measure: Failure containment with no degradation to other modules or core system
architectural_decisions: Modular architecture with plugin isolation
Independent module execution contexts preventing cascading failures
Core system resilience to plugin failures
analysis_records.reasoning: Module isolation requires process-level separation, exception handling boundaries, or resource quotas to prevent failure propagation. The 'no degradation' requirement means other modules must maintain full functionality and performance despite the failed module. This demands careful design of inter-module communication and shared resource management.

risks.description: Shared database connections or resources may propagate failures across modules
risks.rationale: If failed module holds database locks or exhausts connection pools, other modules could experience degradation despite isolation

risks.description: Insufficient error handling in inter-module communication could cause cascading failures
risks.rationale: If modules communicate synchronously without timeout or circuit breaker patterns, a hung module could block dependent modules

risks.description: Memory leaks in failed module may degrade overall system performance
risks.rationale: If module isolation doesn't include resource cleanup, crashed modules could leave allocated memory unreleased, eventually impacting system-wide performance

non_risks.description: Modular architecture design explicitly supports failure isolation
non_risks.rationale: Plugin-based architecture with module boundaries enables independent failure domains

sensitivity_points.parameter: Module isolation mechanism strength (process vs. thread vs. logical)

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (stronger isolation improves failure containment)
Performance (process-level isolation introduces IPC overhead vs. in-process modules)
Reliability (loose coupling prevents cascading failures)
Modifiability (tight coupling enables richer feature integration between modules)
